sections.heading: Follow-Up
sections.content: Follow-up for cutaneous lymphomas emphasizes regular clinical assessment and monitoring for disease progression or relapse. For indolent PC-BCL (PCFCL and PCMZL), after initial treatment, observation is recommended with no routine imaging unless clinically indicated. Imaging (FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT) may be repeated at the end of treatment to assess response or if there is clinical suspicion of progression [20]. For MF/SS, follow-up includes periodic skin examinations, assessment of body surface area (BSA) involvement, and monitoring for extracutaneous disease. Patients with advanced-stage disease or those on systemic therapy require more frequent follow-up. The guidelines do not specify a fixed follow-up schedule, but it is generally recommended to assess response and manage symptoms at each visit. For LyP, lifelong follow-up with thorough skin examination is warranted due to the high risk of second lymphoid malignancies (e.g., MF, PC-ALCL) [21]. Late effects screening should include monitoring for treatment-related toxicities (e.g., neuropathy from brentuximab vedotin, metabolic effects from bexarotene) and secondary malignancies, particularly skin cancers in patients who have received phototherapy or radiation. Recurrence patterns vary by disease: PC-BCL relapses are common in the skin (50% for PCMZL, 30% for PCFCL) but rarely extracutaneous [2]. MF/SS relapses are frequent after therapy discontinuation, and decisions to continue or switch therapy are guided by clinical assessment of the dominant disease compartment [22].

timeline.title: Indolent PC-BCL (PCFCL and PCMZL)
timeline.body: After initial treatment, observation is recommended with no routine imaging unless clinically indicated. Imaging (FDG-PET/CT or C/A/P CT) may be repeated at the end of treatment to assess response or if there is clinical suspicion of progression [20].

timeline.title: MF/SS
timeline.body: Follow-up includes periodic skin examinations, assessment of body surface area (BSA) involvement, and monitoring for extracutaneous disease. Patients with advanced-stage disease or those on systemic therapy require more frequent follow-up. The guidelines do not specify a fixed follow-up schedule, but it is generally recommended to assess response and manage symptoms at each visit.

timeline.title: LyP (Lymphomatoid Papulosis)
timeline.body: Lifelong follow-up with thorough skin examination is warranted due to the high risk of second lymphoid malignancies (e.g., MF, PC-ALCL) [21].

MF/SS relapses are frequent after therapy discontinuation, and decisions to continue or switch therapy are guided by clinical assessment of the dominant disease compartment [22].
